speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three of our interview with Mr. Max Sitzer. This is June 30, 1995. I'm David [? Leipovich, ?] and we're in Toronto. Mr. Sitzer, if you could continue to tell us about what your experiences were in the Russian military after the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: How it felt to be in the military at that time?

words.text: in the military at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the military. First of all, let me-- let me tell you why I did go into the military. I already said, previous this, my-- the first day, my-- my sister got spit in her face. And when she came back, it really hurt me. It hurt me because she was crying, and you know. And at that day, I said to myself, unless I die in-- [PAUSES FOR 27 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and in '44, I had a chance. My father says, you know this. You're not going to return. You 99%, you gonna die. I said, Dad, if that's to be, that's to be. I'm goin' die. I promised myself I'm goin' do it. And if-- and if I'm goin' die, I'm goin' die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember, came in, they took me there. And right away, we had to walk. We had to walk maybe 120 kilometers. My father found out this, I really went. He caught me in the middle of the-- maybe 60 kilometers from the place where I got drafted. It's near-- between Rovno and [? Birastatchko ?]. And he says, maybe you'll change. I said, I'm not changing my mind. He brought me in a whole-- a whole-- not a suitcase, but a whole bag with food. So I-- so I took the food, said goodbye to him, and that's the last time I seen him till I came to Canada, 13 years later. Didn't know what happened. So then they-- I went there. And in Rovno, we came in. And we-- the German start dropping bombs. Terrible. I thought I will never live it through. They hung those-- like-- with those flares or whatever else, and they were bombin' us. We survived. They loaded us in trains and took us to Smolensk. In Smolensk-- in Smolensk, they took us to a-- they have a camp. But it wasn't a camp. It was only under tents. And there, we trained for six months. Mines-- taking out mines, setting up mines. And-- and then, after six months, they shipped us-- they shipped us to Krakow. And near Krakow, we were waiting. They told us in January it's gonna be a big offensive. And I couldn't wait, you know? I want to see action because-- so in January, 1945, before we walked into Germany, a captain took us together, and he says, look boys. We crossing now the German border. We going in not as liberators. We going in as an occupation force. And they got to feel this, we are-- that-- that we are an occupation force. Why did he say that? His parents got hung in Belarusia right in the middle of the town. So we got in. I was young. I was 19 years of age at that time, not quite 20. I was gonna turn 20 in May, and that was in January, beginning of February. Got into the town. So I organized, and we burned, We burned the town right from one end to the other end, the German town. So-- and you know, in-- I remember that word. The Germans have a saying that's [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Or you know, what do you-- how do you say-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how do you say that in English? When you-- I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The payback-- OK, we'll do it that way. You see, and then, I start remembering what they did to us from 1941 till 1944. So I tried to do exactly that what they did to us. Exactly-- it wasn't the time, and I didn't have all the-- the chances to, but I did-- I did what I could. And in the Russian Army, to get in, I actually believed in the system. I was young. The-- the Germans-- I I was nobody. And after a while, when I got in, I was a mensch. I was-- they gave me-- they-- they put me in charge. They-- they-- I-- I, at one time, I was in charge of a prison of war camp. I had maybe 500 prisoners of war, you know? And I don't know why, but I was always lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Either I got a big mouth or I don't know. But I talked myself into. In political, I-- I knew how to-- I knew the system. I found out the system very well. So actually, in the Russian Army, I felt pretty good till they found out that my father's in Canada. And that, at the time, that they took me there. They took me right out, send me to Russia, first to first, through Moscow, then to-- what I-- and then to-- it's called [RUSSIAN]. That's close to [? Rau ?]. That's not to far from Siberia. And there, I spent six months, and I got discharged. That all my career in the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I came in. I start working. Like I said, I-- at first of all, I was doing a job. I had a warehouse. The farmers used to bring the hides to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like pelts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hides. No, a warehouse, had a big warehouse. They used to deliver the hides, like, from cows, from all kind-- you know, cow-- cow hides and wool, raw. I used to have workers, used to sort them, what kind of-- to find out what they are. You know, when they have a little bit cuts, already seconds. And then salt them and-- and then ship them-- ship them back to Lwow. And that, I was there for a year and a half. But I got fed up with that work, left the town, went to Lwow, met my wife, Fannie, and I married her. And then I became-- I found a job as a head of a department store, was there for a-- for a while. Then, I had a store on a farmer's market, and I made pretty good money, not exactly 100% maybe legitimate, but I made good money. And in '56, when-- when Gomulka came to power in Poland, and he repatriate all the Polish citizen from Russia back to Poland. So I went to Kiev, got my papers, and six weeks later, I was in Poland. And from Poland was no problem. So my father was in Canada. He made the papers. I was a year-- I had to be a year in Poland. I could have left before, but my wife took sick, an she couldn't leave. So I had that time, one child, one son. The other son was born here. And I left for Canada after a year. In December '56, I came into Poland. In December '57, I left for Canada. Came in here, I didn't know the language. So I worked manual work. And then my father bought a hotel. Start working for my father, and then I bought him out when he retired. I took over the hotel. And I managed. I was there for 30 years on Green and Broadview. And after-- you know, in-- a few years ago, I retired. I-- I semi-retired. I have a little finance company now, which-- [INAUDIBLE] the mortgage business. I got two-- two sons, seven grandchildren--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sons' names? Leon, L-E-O-N, and the other one is William. And Leon is the oldest. He's born in '52 in Russia. And William was born in-- in '64 in Canada. It's quite a gap, 12 years difference. And he has two kids, a boy and a girl. And Leon has four girls and one boy. So that's how I have seven grandchildren. And we doing a lot of traveling in Canada. I have a place in Florida. I have a place in Israel. And we're OK. We're not multi, multi but we gettin' by. Can you-- just to-- to wrap this, can you tell us in your own words, what your experiences during the Holocaust have meant to your life? What-- what would you want--

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. It's-- my life is-- it will never be the same. First of all, I was very close to my mother, not so much to my father. Not so much to-- the only-- the closest person to me was my mom. And I don't know why. Maybe I was the only boy, you know. I was-- and I had four sisters. And you know, they ruined my life. They ruined my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took away from me-- and I'm not counting the-- the distant relatives, but my immediate family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came here, I didn't have nobody. I made my son's bar mitzvah, the oldest son. I did-- I had a problem to find somebody to call in. And I'll never-- I-- I just can't get-- I couldn't get it over. And I can't get it over. I am very emotional, very-- I remember an incident in Germany-- I go back a little bit. Was-- that woman was an elderly woman, an elderly because I was 20 or 21, and she was-- she was maybe 45 or 50. So she-- she says to me, you only a young man-- she spoke English, and she spoke-- she knew a little Polish, too. But I spoke to her in German. She says, what did they do to you that you have such a unlimited hate to the Germans? I says, you're right. The hate is-- is no-- no-- no limit what I could do to you. There's no limit.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I used to go-- when I was-- when I just came into Germany, I was a wild animal. I was actually-- I didn't-- I don't believe it today how I could do the things which I did. But, at the time, I was-- I was-- I was happy. I did-- It didn't bother me. It didn't bother me at all. And I don't have any regrets, which I did it because, what they did-- I mean-- and I was in the-- the-- It was a lot of people-- my friends went through a lot more because I never seen a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father, you see-- thanks to my father, he-- he kept me always, you know, more or less, so I didn't see a ghetto. I didn't see a camp, concentration camp. They demand-- at one time, they had to send somebody, so my father made sure I am not-- I wasn't going there, you know? Somebody else went, but-- so and that's-- and that's what the-- what the Holocaust did it to me. Thank you very much for the interview, sir.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Starting from the left, myself, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Max Sitzer, Lea Sitzer, standing up, Walerka Sitzer, holding on-- onto her is Edzia Sitzer, David Sitzer, Helena Sitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is more or less 65 years, or 67 years old, It's 1928, 1929. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] David Sitzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was that photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken-- I'll tell you exactly-- in Germany '47 or '48. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Lea Lieberman Sitzer. It's my mother. And in that picture, she was about 18 years, 19 years old. And this picture was given to me by my father. I don't know how he got it. And that's the only picture-- actually the individual I have of my mother, which I was very close to my mom. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a close up of the same picture. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] Now, from the left site is Henia Lieberman. And-- and my-- that's my aunt and my uncle Leon Lieberman. That's my mother's brother. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Jetta Lieberman. She's my cousin, and she's the sister of Feliks Lieberman. She married just in 1938 or '39. And during the Holocaust, her husband got-- they hung her husband and the-- and the thing busted. So he was a lawyer, and he says, according to the international law, if the-- the-- how do you call that? Gallows?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gallow busted, he says, you should let me go. They didn't let him go. They hang him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Bick. Bick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bick?

speaker: interviewee
text: His first name-- his first name, I don't know. B-I-C-K. You know the pens Bic? Exactly spelled his name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Feliks Lieberman. He is my cousin, and he's the brother of Jetta Lieberman. He survived, and he passed away about nine-- nine, 10 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is Walerka Sitzer. She is my sister. And she, in-- she went on-- on a phony birth certificate to Germany and then worked in a factory. And in '44, she committed suicide for an unknown reason to me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is from the left site, Nastja Woloszyn That's the wife from Jakob Woloszyn. She hid us. And that's her daughter, Sofa-- Sofia. They-- she is still alive and lives in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken approximately 12 years ago. So now, it's 1995. Maybe in '82, '83. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's Max Sitzer, [LAUGHS] myself. And it was taken in May 1945. I was 20 years of age at that time. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] From my right is my oldest son, Leon, and his family. They--

speaker: interviewee
text: Devon.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Devon ?] Sitzer, Carrie Sitzer, [? Fallon ?] Sitzer, Greggie Sitzer, and Stephanie Sitzer. From my left is my younger son, William, his wife, Carol, my daughter-in-law, and her-- her little girl-- my little granddaughter, Rebecca. And my wife, Fannie, and my little grandson, David, and myself, Max Sitzer. Guess I got everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi!

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, everybody!

speaker: interviewee
text: All righty then.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's so funny? [? FALLON SITZER: ?] Greg.
